news.title: Middle East Conflict Escalates as Iran–Israel Attacks Intensify, Ceasefire Remains Elusive
news.text: The ongoing Iran–Israel conflict continues to escalate with fresh attacks across Iran, Israel, and Lebanon, and no sign of a ceasefire. Israel struck key Iranian infrastructure, while Iran launched missiles at Israel and the UAE. Donald Trump delayed military action and set a deadline for reopening the Strait of Hormuz. Despite claiming military success, tensions remain high. Iran condemned attacks at the UN, while concerns grow over nuclear safety and troop deployments. Israel also faces internal strain due to manpower shortages, highlighting the widening impact of the conflict.

news.title: NATO Sees Sharp Increase in Europe and Canada's Defense Spending
news.text: NATO’s European allies and Canada increased defense spending by 20% in 2025, with all members meeting or exceeding the 2% GDP target set in 2014. NATO Secretary General Mark Rutte urged countries to move toward a new goal of 5% of GDP on defense by 2035. Donald Trump continued to pressure allies to spend more and criticized their contributions. While some countries already exceed 3.5%, others remain at 2%. Overall, NATO spending reached 2.77% of GDP, with the United States contributing about 60%, highlighting imbalance.

news.title: Philippines declares national energy emergency as Asia risks energy crisis amid Iran war
news.text: The Philippines declared a national energy emergency due to disruptions caused by the U.S.-Israeli conflict with Iran, which has affected global oil supply. President Ferdinand Marcos Jr. warned of risks to energy stability, while countries like South Korea, Japan, and others introduced energy-saving measures. The crisis worsened after the closure of the Strait of Hormuz, a key route for global oil transport. The International Energy Agency called it a major global threat. Rising oil prices and supply disruptions heavily impact Asia, which depends on imports, prompting emergency actions worldwide.

news.title: Amid West Asia Crisis, India's Oil Reserves Can Last For Just Six Days: Report
news.text: India faces concerns over energy security as its strategic petroleum reserves hold only about six days of crude amid disruptions from tensions near the Strait of Hormuz. Although total capacity could cover around 9.5 days, current storage is only about 64% full. Despite earlier budget allocations to expand reserves, actual spending has been much lower. With nearly 40% of imports passing through the strait and over 88% of crude being imported overall, the situation highlights India’s vulnerability to global supply shocks and the urgent need to strengthen energy reserves.

news.title: Asian stocks mostly fall and oil climbs again over Iran war de-escalation uncertaintiese
news.text: Asian markets mostly declined amid uncertainty over the Iran–Israel conflict, while oil prices rose due to supply concerns linked to the Strait of Hormuz. Major indices in Japan, South Korea, Hong Kong, and China fell, though Taiwan saw slight gains. Oil prices climbed, with Brent nearing $98 per barrel, as tensions escalated and Iran rejected a U.S. ceasefire plan under Donald Trump. The conflict has pushed oil prices up about 40%. Meanwhile, Wall Street closed higher, tech stocks surged, and gold, silver, and the U.S. dollar showed mixed movements.

news.title: Medicine prices to rise due to raw material shortage amid West Asia war, warn Himachal pharma manufacturers
news.text: The ongoing West Asia conflict has disrupted the pharmaceutical supply chain in India, sharply increasing raw material costs by 200–300% and cutting supplies by about 40%. Manufacturers, especially in Himachal Pradesh, warn of reduced production, potential job losses, and medicine shortages. Prices of key inputs and drugs like Paracetamol have surged, with further increases expected. Industry bodies have urged government intervention under the Essential Commodities Act and regulation by the National Pharmaceutical Pricing Authority. Without action, medicine prices may rise 20–25%, affecting availability of essential and life-saving drugs.

news.title: Infosys to acquire Optimum Healthcare IT for $465 million
news.text: Infosys has agreed to acquire Optimum Healthcare IT for $465 million to strengthen its healthcare and digital transformation capabilities. Founded in 2012 and based in Florida, Optimum provides consulting and technology services to hospitals and health systems, with FY25 revenues of $275.9 million. The deal will expand Infosys’ presence in the healthcare provider segment and enhance offerings in cloud, AI, cybersecurity, and digital services, including Infosys Topaz and Cobalt. Both companies expect the acquisition to accelerate innovation and growth. The transaction is likely to close in early FY27.

news.title: EC tightens AI content rules, to set up polling booths in high-rise building communities
news.text: The Election Commission of India has issued guidelines for the 2026 Tamil Nadu Legislative Assembly elections 2026, mandating clear labeling and quick removal of misleading AI-generated content. Political ads require prior approval, and new polling stations in residential areas aim to boost voter turnout, ensuring transparency and reducing misinformation.

news.title: Palantir AI to support UK finance operations
news.text: The Financial Conduct Authority is piloting AI tools from Palantir Technologies to detect financial crimes like money laundering, fraud, and insider trading across 42,000 firms. The three-month project analyses vast unstructured data, including emails, calls, and reports, improving enforcement efficiency. The UK is also expanding AI use into defense and national security, with major investments and partnerships. Strict data protection rules ensure sensitive information remains under government control, with Palantir acting only as a data processor. The initiative highlights AI’s growing role in enhancing regulatory oversight and national security operations.
